aliases: தொடை மற்றும் தோல் கொண்ட வறுத்த கோழி

tamil: தொடை மற்றும் தோல் கொண்ட வறுத்த கோழி

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for battered deep-fried chicken as a prepared dish; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of 2 pieces of fried chicken weighs approximately 200 g, representing a standard meal portion commonly served at hawker centres and fast-food outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Chicken (2 pc) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Chicken (2 pc), CR ID F11020029. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 6.3 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central reference for fried chicken with skin (USDA SR Legacy item 05062) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore-style battered fried chicken (2 pc). GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous entry for battered/breaded fried chicken from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables. The batter coating contributes moderate carbohydrate but the high protein and fat content of the dish substantially blunts the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (6.42 g/100g × 200 g serving = 12.84 g) / 100 = 5.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 553 mg/100g; a 2-piece serving (≈200 g) delivers approximately 1,106 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid fried chicken due to its very high sodium load; if consumed, restrict to one piece, remove the skin to reduce saturated fat, and avoid salty dipping sauces or marinades.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (≈45) and low sugar (0.36 g/100g) are favourable, but high total fat (12.25 g/100g) and deep-frying method promote insulin resistance and excess caloric intake over time.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried chicken may be consumed occasionally by individuals with type 2 diabetes given its low GI and negligible sugar, but the 2-piece portion substantially increases total fat and caloric load; grilled or baked chicken is strongly preferred, and sweet dipping sauces must be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combination of high sodium (553 mg/100g), saturated fat (~3.4 g/100g), trans fat (0.3 g/100g), and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) generated by deep-frying collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit deep-fried chicken; the trans fat content, high sodium, and AGE load from deep-frying are all independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk, and a 2-piece serving amplifies this exposure significantly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trans fat at 0.3 g/100g and saturated fat above 3 g/100g further promote systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried chicken as the AGE load, trans fat, and saturated fat content are all pro-inflammatory; grilled or baked skinless chicken is the recommended alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken (2 Pieces) is a popular deep-fried chicken dish consisting of two bone-in pieces — typically a drumstick and thigh or two drumsticks — coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and submerged in hot oil until golden and crispy. It is a staple at Singapore hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and fast-food outlets, and is widely consumed across all ethnic communities in Singapore. The exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned, while the interior remains juicy and tender. Per 100 g, the dish provides 215 kcal, 19.75 g protein, 12.25 g fat, 6.42 g carbohydrate, 0.36 g sugar, 0.66 g dietary fibre, and 553 mg sodium. The high protein content makes it a filling protein source, but the elevated sodium (553 mg/100g) and fat from deep-frying raise clinical concerns for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The 2-piece portion approximately doubles the absolute sodium and fat intake compared to a single piece, making portion control critical. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), driven by the protein and fat content blunting glucose absorption despite the batter coating. Deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and small amounts of trans fat, both of which are pro-inflammatory. This dish is suitable for halal diets and contains gluten from the wheat flour batter. It is commonly served as a main dish at breakfast, lunch, or dinner in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in 2 pieces of fried chicken?
Is fried chicken suitable for diabetics?
Sodium content of fried chicken Singapore
Can I eat fried chicken with high blood pressure?
Fried chicken fat content Singapore hawker

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a confidence of 0.95 for an exact match to this dish. Saturated fat is adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly flagged as such.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.4 g/100g) is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken with skin (item 05062) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This is an approximation and should be updated when HPB publishes disaggregated fatty acid data. 2. Trans fat (0.3 g/100g) is carried forward from the input record health_suitability notes; this value is an estimate based on typical deep-frying trans fat generation and is not directly reported by HPB. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this item, and no sufficiently close approved-source equivalent was available to provide reliable estimates for all fields. 4. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' from the input record has been removed as it is not supported by the food description — the batter is standard wheat flour, not wholegrain. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (≈45, low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this specific Singapore-style preparation was found. 6. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for 2 average-sized fried chicken pieces; actual weight may vary by outlet and cut.
